signals.text: CFPB consent order May 2024: Chime ordered to pay $1.3M in consumer redress plus $3.25M civil money penalty for delaying refunds to customers after account closures — in some cases leaving people without access to their money for weeks.
California DFPI consent order February 2024: Chime required to pay $2.5M penalty for mishandling consumer complaints during COVID-19 pandemic (2021).
2021 state regulatory settlements (California DFPI and Illinois DFPR): Chime's marketing implied it was a bank when it is not. Required to change marketing practices.

Data breach April 2026: Team 313 (Iran-linked cybercriminal group) attacked Chime's servers. Approximately 20,000 users locked out of accounts during outage. Sensitive PII potentially exposed. Class action filed April 3, 2026 in N.D. California.

Account freeze class action (2020-2024): Chime abruptly closed thousands of accounts without adequate notice or timely return of balances. Settlement negotiations active as of early 2025. Estimated payouts $50-$500 per claimant.

CFPB complaint volume: Chime described as 'one of the most complained-about fintech companies' in the CFPB database. BBB: 8,000+ complaints in 3 years despite A+ rating. Systemic customer service failures.

No banking charter: Chime is not a bank. Services depend entirely on partner banks (Bancorp Bank, Stride Bank). If either partner ends the relationship or faces its own regulatory action, Chime's entire product collapses. Structural dependency risk.

IPO valuation decline: Chime went public June 2025 at $27/share (~$11.6B-$18.4B valuation) — significantly below $25B peak private valuation in 2021. Suggests investor skepticism about growth trajectory.

not_findable_from_free_sources: Precise number of affected accounts in CFPB action
Bancorp/Stride contract terms and renewal dates
